card.intuition: Accessible Rich Internet Applications (ARIA) is a set of attributes that you add to HTML to describe the purpose and state of elements to assistive technologies (like screen readers).
card.analogy: Closed captioning for the UI: if you build a custom 'checkbox' out of a `<div>`, a screen reader doesn't know it's clickable. By adding `role="checkbox"`, you are giving the screen reader the 'script' so it can tell the user what's happening.
when_to_use: Creating custom UI widgets (drawers, tabs, sliders) that don't have native HTML equivalents
Labeling interactive elements that don't have visible text (like an 'X' button for closing)
Announcing dynamic content changes to screen readers using `aria-live`
gotchas: The First Rule of ARIA: If you can use a native HTML element (like `<button>`) instead of ARIA (like `<div role="button">`), DO IT. Native elements have accessibility built-in for free
Incorrect ARIA is often worse than NO ARIA, as it can confuse assistive technologies

exercise.prompt: Identify the attribute used to tell a screen reader that a section was updated dynamically.
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.lower() == "live"
assert check("live")
print("PASS")
